step_by_step_thinking:
The patients likely suffered from various types of traumatic injuries caused by the blast. Explosions typically cause non-directional force that can result in thermal (burns), blunt trauma like bruises and lacerations, as well as penetrating or fracturing effects depending on proximity to shrapnel or debris. Option D mentions bruising, laceration, and fracture as an exclusive "triad" indicative of all explosive injury cases, whereas other patterns may be more common but not universal for every case involving explosives.

My Answer: **D** Based on my clinical understanding, while these signs often occur with explosive injuries, they do not form an absolute triad applicable to every single explosive incident; hence
raw_response:
The patients likely suffered from various types of traumatic injuries caused by the blast. Explosions typically cause non-directional force that can result in thermal (burns), blunt trauma like bruises and lacerations, as well as penetrating or fracturing effects depending on proximity to shrapnel or debris. Option D mentions bruising, laceration, and fracture as an exclusive "triad" indicative of all explosive injury cases, whereas other patterns may be more common but not universal for every case involving explosives.

My Answer: **D** Based on my clinical understanding, while these signs often occur with explosive injuries, they do not form an absolute triad applicable to every single explosive incident; hence option D stands out as potentially incorrect compared to others describing general characteristics of blast injuries.
